generated_comment: Karan: Yaar, it's a great opportunity, I'm not saying it isn't. But I keep telling her — at this point in our lives, with Amma's BP and everything, and we had a plan, you know? One year feels like nothing on paper but it's not nothing. And honestly I think she knows it too, deep down. It's just... who's going to hold all of this together if she goes? I can't do my job and manage the house and parents and also be doing long distance. She's not thinking about any of that. Brother: But da, didn't she say she'd figure out arrangements? Karan: She says that, but 'figuring out' means it still falls on me to execute, no? I'm just asking her to be realistic. I'm not stopping her. I'm asking her to think about whether this is the right time.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Karan presents himself as reasonable and supportive while systematically reframing Meera's professional achievement as a logistical burden she is irresponsibly offloading onto the family. The comment encodes the career-vs-family binary by implying that managing the household is her default responsibility, and that pursuing her career ambition at this moment constitutes a failure of her domestic role — without applying the same standard to his own career continuity.
persona_text: You are Karan. Age 32. Married for three years. A man in Bengaluru. Your wife Meera has just been shortlisted for a competitive one year fellowship in the United States. It is a significant recognition of her work. She is excited and nervous. You are not against her going in principle. But over the past week of discussing it you have raised so many practical concerns Ñ the apartment, who manages things here, your parents' health, whether long distance for a year is realistic, what happens to your plans to start a family this year Ñ that the conversation has slowly shifted from whether she should go to whether she can manage all the things that would need to be managed if she goes. You are on the phone with your brother tonight telling him about the fellowship and the conversation you have been having with Meera and where things stand.
